title: VDOT Framework
description: Jack Daniels' VDOT system quantifies aerobic fitness as the V̇O₂max that produces a given race performance. Used to set training paces across all intensity zones.

title: VO2max Development
description: VO2max is the maximum rate of oxygen consumption. Interval training at 95–100% of VO2max pace (≈3–5 min efforts) provides the strongest stimulus for improvement.

title: Lactate Threshold
description: The highest intensity sustainable without significant lactate accumulation. Threshold training (20–40 min at T-pace) raises this ceiling, directly improving marathon and half-marathon performance.

title: Aerobic Base Building
description: Foundation of endurance training. 8–16 weeks of high-volume easy running builds mitochondrial density, capillary networks, and fat oxidation capacity before intensity is introduced.

title: Periodisation Principles
description: Structured training cycles (mesocycles of 3 weeks hard + 1 week recovery) prevent overtraining and allow supercompensation. Macrocycle aligns peak fitness with race day.

title: Training Stress Balance
description: Chronic Training Load (CTL) minus Acute Training Load (ATL) equals Form (TSB). Optimal race performance occurs when Form is +5 to +25 after a taper.

title: Easy Run Prescription
description: 80% of training volume should be at easy/conversational pace (Z1–Z2). Keeps stress low, allows adaptation, and builds aerobic efficiency without excessive fatigue.

title: Interval Training
description: Repeated high-intensity efforts with recovery intervals. I-pace intervals (3–5 min) target VO2max; R-pace repetitions (under 2 min) develop speed and economy.

title: Long Run Science
description: Weekly long run (≥90 min) builds glycogen storage capacity, fat oxidation, and mental toughness. Progressive overload: no more than 10% weekly distance increase.

title: Marathon-Pace Segments
description: Including M-pace (marathon pace) miles in long runs teaches the body and mind to hold race effort while fatigued. Typically last 20–40% of a long run.

title: Strides
description: Short (20–30s) accelerations at R-pace with full recovery. Improve running economy, neuromuscular activation, and form without significant aerobic stress. 4–6 strides 2–3×/week.

title: Recovery Runs
description: Very easy runs (Z1, below 70% max HR) the day after hard workouts. Flush metabolic waste, maintain training volume, and reinforce aerobic adaptation without adding stress.

title: Progression Runs
description: Runs beginning easy and finishing at M-pace or faster. Build metabolic efficiency and practice pacing discipline. Typically 10–16 km.

title: Fartlek Training
description: Unstructured speed play: varying pace by feel or terrain. Bridges gap between easy running and structured intervals. Low psychological stress with aerobic/anaerobic benefit.

title: Cross-Training Equivalence
description: Cycling, swimming, elliptical maintain aerobic fitness with lower impact. Cycling effort equivalent: multiply running duration by 1.4. Useful during injury or taper.

title: Heat Acclimatisation
description: Running in heat forces plasma volume expansion and improved sweating response. 10–14 days of moderate heat exposure adds ~5% plasma volume, benefiting performance in any climate.

title: Altitude Training
description: At altitude (>1500m), reduced O₂ partial pressure stimulates EPO production, increasing red blood cell mass. Live high/train low is optimal. Benefits persist 3–4 weeks post-descent.

title: Cardiac Drift
description: In long runs, HR gradually rises at constant pace (cardiac drift). Normal up to 5–7%. Greater drift signals dehydration or excess heat stress. Use pace as primary governor in heat.

title: Training Zones
description: Five-zone model anchored by max HR: Z1 <70%, Z2 70–80%, Z3 80–87%, Z4 87–93%, Z5 >93%. Zone 2 builds aerobic base; zone 4–5 drive performance improvements.

title: Supercompensation
description: After training stress + adequate recovery, fitness rises above baseline. Window is 48–72h post-hard-session. Timing the next hard workout to this window maximises adaptation.

title: Tempo Running
description: Sustained effort at lactate threshold (roughly 10K race pace for recreational runners). 20–40 min continuous or 2×15 min. The single most effective session for marathon pace improvement.

title: VO2max Intervals
description: 800m–1600m repeats at current 3K–5K race pace with equal recovery. 5–6 reps per session. Elevates VO2max ceiling, improves aerobic power.

title: Running Economy
description: Oxygen cost at a given pace. Improved by strength training, plyometrics, strides, and mileage accumulation. A 5% economy gain equals roughly 5% race improvement at same VO2max.

title: Tapering
description: Reducing training volume 2–3 weeks before race while maintaining intensity. Allows full glycogen replenishment, tissue repair, and neuromuscular freshness. Do not add new workouts.

title: Overtraining Syndrome
description: Chronic imbalance of stress and recovery. Symptoms: persistent fatigue, elevated resting HR, mood disturbance, performance decline. Requires 2–8 weeks full rest. Prevention > cure.

title: Shin Splints (MTSS)
description: Medial tibial stress syndrome: pain along the inner shin. Caused by rapid mileage increase, hard surfaces, or worn shoes. Treat with rest, ice, and gradual return. Address gait and footwear.

title: IT Band Syndrome
description: Iliotibial band friction syndrome: lateral knee pain. Caused by hip weakness, excessive downhill running, or sudden mileage increase. Treat: hip strengthening, foam rolling, reduce downhill.

title: Plantar Fasciitis
description: Heel/arch pain from plantar fascia overload. Aggravated by tight calves and insufficient arch support. Morning pain is hallmark. Treat: calf stretching, foot strengthening, supportive footwear.

title: Achilles Tendinopathy
description: Overuse injury of the Achilles tendon. Insertional (at heel bone) vs mid-portion. Best treated with eccentric calf raises (Alfredson protocol). Avoid stretching acute Achilles.

title: Stress Fractures
description: Bone stress injuries from repetitive loading exceeding remodelling capacity. Common sites: tibia, metatarsals, navicular. Navicular and femoral neck are high-risk (no running). Requires imaging.

title: Runner's Knee (PFPS)
description: Patellofemoral pain syndrome: diffuse anterior knee pain. Caused by quad weakness, hip weakness, or excessive pronation. Treatment: VMO strengthening, hip abductor work.

title: Hamstring Strain
description: Grade 1–3 tears of the hamstring, usually at the proximal insertion or muscle belly. Common in speed sessions. Prevention: Nordic curls, progressive speed buildup, warm-up.

title: Calf Strain
description: Tear in gastrocnemius or soleus. Sudden sharp pain ('pull'). Grade 1: mild; Grade 3: complete rupture. RICE protocol; eccentric loading in rehabilitation.

title: 10% Rule
description: Increase weekly training volume by no more than 10% per week. Prevents cumulative load exceeding the musculoskeletal system's adaptation rate. Critical after a training break.

title: Strength Training for Runners
description: Hip, glute, and single-leg strength directly reduces injury risk. Key exercises: single-leg squats, hip thrusts, calf raises, Copenhagen planks. 2×/week is sufficient.

title: Foam Rolling
description: Self-myofascial release reduces perceived tightness and may improve short-term range of motion. Not a replacement for warming up or strength. Most effective post-run.

title: Shoe Rotation
description: Rotating between two pairs of shoes (different stack heights/drop) allows midsole foam recovery and varies load distribution. Reduces injury risk vs single-shoe use.

title: Gait Analysis
description: Identifying biomechanical issues (overstriding, excessive pronation, hip drop) that increase injury risk. Video analysis at 60fps minimum. Interventions: cueing, drills, footwear, insoles.

title: Warm-Up Protocol
description: Dynamic warm-up (leg swings, hip circles, A-skips, B-skips, 5 min easy jog) reduces injury risk before hard sessions. Never stretch cold muscles statically before a run.

title: Return to Run Protocol
description: After injury, progress: walking → walk/run intervals → easy running → tempo. Each stage 3–7 days. Return to full training takes 2–4× the lay-off period.

title: Sleep and Injury Risk
description: Athletes sleeping <6h/night have 1.7× higher injury risk (Milewski et al). Immune function, tissue repair, and HGH release peak during deep sleep. 8–9h is optimal for athletes.

title: Bone Health and Stress Reactions
description: Low bone density (common in athletes with RED-S) dramatically increases stress fracture risk. DEXA scanning indicated for recurrent bone stress injuries. Address nutrition first.

title: Hip Flexor Tightness
description: Tight psoas/iliopsoas limits hip extension and increases anterior pelvic tilt, loading the lower back and hamstrings. Address with hip flexor stretches post-run and hip mobility work.

title: Piriformis Syndrome
description: Piriformis muscle irritates sciatic nerve, causing buttock/hip pain that mimics disc pathology. Common in runners with weak external hip rotators. Piriformis stretches + hip strengthening.

title: Blisters and Skin Care
description: Friction blisters from moisture + rubbing. Prevention: technical socks, BodyGlide/Vaseline on hot spots, well-fitted shoes. Pop large blisters with sterile needle, leave roof intact.

title: Ankle Sprains
description: Grade 1–3 lateral ankle sprains. PRICE protocol (Protection, Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation). Proprioceptive rehabilitation essential before return to running.

title: Lower Back Pain in Runners
description: Usually muscular or related to hip/core weakness rather than disc pathology. Excessive anterior pelvic tilt and weak glutes are primary drivers. Core stability and hip strength are key.

title: Toenail Injuries
description: Black toenails from downhill running or tight toe box. Prevention: 0.5cm space in front of longest toe, lace properly for descents. Prevention > treatment.

title: Hydration and Injury Risk
description: Dehydration stiffens tendons and reduces cartilage lubrication, increasing injury susceptibility. Even 2% dehydration impairs proprioception and decision-making during hard sessions.

title: Training Load Monitoring
description: Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio (ACWR) above 1.5 significantly increases injury risk. Keep ACWR 0.8–1.3 for safe training build. Spike in workload = spike in risk.

title: Carbohydrate Fuelling
description: Carbs are the primary fuel above easy pace. Glycogen stores (~90 min at marathon pace) must be maximised before long sessions and races. Aim 6–10g/kg/day on high-training days.

title: Race-Day Nutrition Strategy
description: Pre-race: high-carb meal 3–4h before, small carb snack 1h before. During marathon: 40–60g carbs/hr from 30 min in, every 20–30 min. Post-race: carb+protein within 30 min.

title: Protein for Recovery
description: 1.6–2.2g protein/kg/day for endurance athletes. 20–40g within 30 min post-run maximises muscle protein synthesis. Leucine-rich sources (dairy, whey, eggs) are most anabolic.

title: Fat as Fuel
description: At easy pace (<70% HRmax), 50–60% of energy from fat. High-fat oxidation capacity extends glycogen stores in marathon. Trained runners oxidise ~1g fat/min vs 0.5g in untrained.

title: Electrolyte Management
description: Sodium (500–1000mg/hr), potassium, magnesium lost in sweat. Replace during efforts >90 min or in heat. Hyponatremia (over-drinking plain water) is more dangerous than dehydration in marathons.

title: Sweat Rate Testing
description: Individual sweat rates vary 0.5–2.5L/hr. Test: weigh pre/post run; each 1kg loss ≈ 1L sweat. Aim to replace 80% of losses. Do not overdrink.

title: Gel and Sports Drink Selection
description: Gels: ~25g carbs each. Sports drinks: ~14g carbs/250ml. Mix glucose:fructose 2:1 improves absorption above 60g/hr. Maltodextrin + fructose blends recommended for long efforts.

title: Gut Training
description: GI distress during races is common but trainable. Practice consuming carbs at race intensity in training. Begin with 30g/hr, progress to 60–90g/hr over weeks.

title: Iron and Endurance Performance
description: Iron deficiency (common in female runners, vegetarians, high-mileage athletes) impairs O₂ transport. Symptoms: fatigue, pale skin, elevated HR. Monitor ferritin; aim >40ng/mL.

title: Caffeine for Performance
description: 3–6mg/kg body weight 30–60 min pre-race improves endurance performance by ~3%. Adenosine receptor antagonist. Side effects: GI upset, anxiety in sensitive athletes. Do not try race-day for first time.

title: Vitamin D and Athletic Performance
description: Deficiency widespread in athletes, especially indoors or northern latitudes. Affects bone health, immune function, and muscle function. Target 40–60ng/mL. Supplement 2000–4000 IU/day in winter.

title: Post-Run Recovery Nutrition
description: Golden window: 30–60 min post-run. Consume 0.8g carbs/kg + 20–40g protein. Chocolate milk is evidence-backed. Speeds glycogen resynthesis and muscle repair.

title: RED-S (Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport)
description: Chronic energy deficit in athletes. Impairs bone density, hormonal function, immune system, and performance. More common in female athletes. Address via dietitian.

title: Carbohydrate Loading
description: Protocol for marathon and longer: 3 days of high-carb intake (10–12g/kg/day) combined with taper. Increases muscle glycogen stores by 20–30%. Expect 1–2kg weight gain from water.

title: Beetroot/Nitrate Supplementation
description: Dietary nitrates (beetroot juice) convert to nitric oxide, improving muscle efficiency by ~2%. Dose: 300–600mg nitrate, 2–3h before effort. Acute and chronic supplementation both effective.

title: Creatine for Endurance Athletes
description: Not traditionally endurance-focused, but creatine aids sprint and strength elements of training, and may improve recovery. 3–5g/day maintenance. May cause 1–2kg weight gain initially.

title: Hydration Strategy
description: General: 400–800ml/hr during running, adjusted for conditions. Drink to thirst in training; for races >90 min follow a plan. Clear to pale-yellow urine indicates adequate hydration.

title: Pre-Run Meal Timing
description: Full meal 3–4h before; snack 1–1.5h before. High-fat/high-fibre foods before running risk GI distress. Avoid high-FODMAP foods the night before long runs.

title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids
description: EPA and DHA reduce exercise-induced inflammation and may enhance muscle protein synthesis. 2–4g/day from fish oil. Important for vegetarian/vegan athletes who lack dietary sources.

title: Alcohol and Recovery
description: Even moderate alcohol (>0.5g/kg) within 4h of exercise significantly impairs muscle protein synthesis, glycogen storage, and sleep quality. Delay consumption until recovery is well established.

title: Sleep Optimisation
description: 8–9h of sleep is optimal for athletes. Growth hormone and testosterone peak during slow-wave sleep. Consistent sleep/wake times amplify recovery. Blue light restriction 1–2h before bed.

title: Active Recovery
description: Low-intensity movement (easy walk, swim, or Z1 run at <65% HRmax) the day after a hard session flushes metabolic waste and reduces DOMS more effectively than passive rest.

title: Ice Baths and Cold Water Immersion
description: 10–15 min at 10–15°C reduces acute inflammation and perceived soreness. Best used after competitions, not after strength training (blunts hypertrophy signal). 2–4h post-exercise is optimal timing.

title: Compression Garments
description: Compression socks/tights post-exercise reduce swelling and perceived soreness by 10–15% in studies. Particularly useful for travel after races. Wear for 2–4h post-run.

title: Massage for Recovery
description: Sports massage 24–48h post-hard-session reduces DOMS and perceived tightness. Does not speed physiological recovery but improves psychological readiness. Not recommended immediately post-race.

title: HRV Monitoring
description: Heart Rate Variability reflects autonomic nervous system recovery. Low HRV (vs personal baseline) indicates incomplete recovery. Measure first thing in morning before rising. Apps: HRV4Training, Whoop.

title: Resting Heart Rate Monitoring
description: Elevated resting HR (>7 bpm above normal) is a reliable indicator of incomplete recovery, illness, or overreaching. Measure in the same conditions daily. Trend matters more than individual readings.

title: Deload Weeks
description: Every 3–4 weeks, reduce training volume by 30–40% while maintaining intensity. Allows full tissue repair and supercompensation. Athletes often fear deloads but emerge fitter.

title: Napping for Performance
description: 20–30 min naps improve alertness, reaction time, and endurance performance. Napping 30–90 min before an afternoon or evening session improves output. Keep under 30 min to avoid sleep inertia.

title: Parasympathetic Recovery Techniques
description: Box breathing (4-4-4-4), progressive muscle relaxation, and yoga nidra activate the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing cortisol and accelerating recovery between hard sessions.

title: DOMS Management
description: Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness peaks 24–48h post-unaccustomed exercise. Managed by easy movement, adequate protein, and patience. Not caused by lactic acid. Indicates adaptation stimulus.

title: Sauna and Heat Therapy
description: Post-exercise sauna (15–20 min, >80°C) increases plasma volume, improves heat tolerance, and may increase EPO. 3–4 sessions/week in training blocks. Rehydrate immediately after.

title: Nutrition Timing in Recovery
description: Immediate post-run window (0–30 min) is critical. 0.8g carbs/kg + 20–40g protein. Second meal within 2h continues glycogen replenishment. Skipping recovery nutrition extends fatigue by 24–48h.

title: Training Load Periodisation
description: Hard day / easy day principle. Never stack two hard sessions back-to-back without an easy day. Weekend long run + quality session should be separated by at least 2 easy days.

title: Cognitive Recovery
description: Mental fatigue from work/life stress impairs physical training quality and recovery rate. Perceived exertion at a given pace increases under mental fatigue. Recovery is holistic.

title: Mobility and Flexibility
description: Post-run static stretching (hold 30–60s) improves long-term range of motion and reduces injury risk. Target calves, hip flexors, hamstrings, piriformis. Not effective as injury prevention in isolation.

title: Leg Elevation
description: Elevating legs (15–20 min above heart level) post-long-run assists venous return and reduces swelling. Simple and effective; combine with hydration and light snack.

title: Training Diary and Recovery Patterns
description: Tracking subjective recovery (1–10 scale), sleep hours, mood, and resting HR reveals personal recovery patterns. Enables data-driven training adjustments before fatigue accumulates.

title: Social Recovery
description: Social connection reduces cortisol and perception of effort. Running clubs, shared long runs, and post-race celebrations contribute to sustainable training habits and psychological recovery.

title: Periodised Recovery
description: Recovery must be built into the macro plan. Key recovery points: after goal races (2–4 weeks), after peak training phases, and at season transitions. Planned rest outperforms reactive rest.

title: Marathon Pacing Strategy
description: Even pacing within 2% produces best results at marathon. Negative split (second half 1–2 min faster) is achievable for well-trained athletes. Going out too fast by >10s/km is a primary failure mode.

title: Half Marathon Race Strategy
description: Aim for even splits. First 5K should feel easy. Commit to race pace at 5K. Hold through 15K. Final 6K is where fitness shows. Average HR should reach 88–92% of max by 10K.

title: Course Reconnaissance
description: Study elevation profile, turns, surface, and typical weather. Know exact fuelling station locations. Identify spectator points for psychological lifts. Walk the finish area if possible.

title: Race-Day Weather Adjustment
description: Optimal marathon temp is 7–12°C. Adjust pace +30s/km per 5°C above 15°C. In heat, HR is a better guide than pace; switch to HR-controlled effort. Hydrate proactively.

title: Fuelling During the Race
description: Start fuelling at 30–45 min, before glycogen depletion begins. 40–60g carbs/hr for <4h finishers; 60–90g/hr for longer efforts. Take gels with water, never sports drink (too much sugar).

title: Hitting the Wall
description: Glycogen depletion ('bonking') at 30–35km. Legs turn heavy, pace slows dramatically. Prevented by adequate carb loading, correct race-day fuelling, and not going out too fast.

title: Race-Day Routine
description: Wake 3–4h before start. Eat known pre-race meal. Arrive 90 min before gun. Warm-up (10–15 min easy jog + strides) for 5K and 10K; minimal warm-up for marathon.

title: Seeding Yourself Correctly
description: Starting in the correct corral prevents the first-km surge from forcing excessive early effort. Seed based on current fitness, not aspirational goal. Starting slow is forgivable; starting fast is not.

title: Running Tangents
description: Always run the shortest legal line (tangents around corners). In a 42.2km marathon, running the wide line adds 200–400m. Focus on inner edges of turns.

title: Drafting
description: Running in the slipstream of another runner reduces air resistance by 5–7% at marathon pace. Legal in road races. Especially valuable in windy conditions. Requires maintaining pace of the group.

title: Post-Race Recovery Timeline
description: Day 1–2: complete rest, aggressive nutrition. Week 1: walking only. Week 2: easy 20–30 min runs. Full training resumes after ~4 weeks for marathon. 'One easy day per race mile' is a useful guide.

title: Cramping in Races
description: Multi-factorial: dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, neuromuscular fatigue, and going too fast. Prevention: adequate training, correct pacing, and electrolyte intake. In-race: slow down, stretch, take sodium.

title: Hill Running Technique
description: Uphill: shorten stride, maintain cadence, drive arms, lean forward slightly. Downhill: controlled lean, mid-foot landing, resist the urge to brake. Downhill 5K effort ≈ flat 5K stress on quads.

title: Psychological Checkpoints
description: Break the race into segments (10K chunks for marathon). Focus on process (cadence, form, pacing) rather than outcome. Positive self-talk at 30K is a critical skill.

title: Pre-Race Taper Madness
description: Common: phantom pains, low energy, doubt, weight gain (glycogen water). All are normal and temporary. Resist urge to do extra workouts. Trust the training.

title: Selecting a Goal Race
description: Choose races with favourable courses, weather history, and timing within training cycle. Peak marathons (Berlin, Chicago, London) have flat courses and optimal October conditions.

title: Qualifying Standards
description: Boston, London, and other majors require time standards. Boston: age-graded qualification times, with rolling cutoffs typically 2–8 min faster than standard. Plan training cycles around qualification windows.

title: Race-Week Preparation
description: Final long run 2–3 weeks before race. Race week: short easy runs only, strides, carb loading Day 3–1. Avoid new foods, new gear, alcohol, and social obligations that disrupt sleep.

title: Pacers and Pace Groups
description: Official pacers take cognitive load off the athlete. Choose the pacer group conservatively. If you feel strong at 30K, you can push away from the group; if struggling, let them go.

title: Age-Graded Performance
description: Age-graded percentages allow fair comparison across ages. 70% is solid club level; 80% is competitive; 90%+ is elite. Useful for goal-setting as athletes age and for comparing progress.

title: Athlete-Centred Coaching
description: Coaching that prioritises the athlete's goals, values, and voice. Coach as facilitator, not director. Leads to greater intrinsic motivation and long-term adherence.

title: SMART Goal Setting
description: Goals should be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. Process goals (complete 90% of sessions) are more within the athlete's control than outcome goals (run 3:00).

title: Behaviour Change Principles
description: Fogg Behaviour Model: behaviour = motivation × ability × prompt. Reduce friction for desired behaviours (lay out kit the night before). Use if-then planning for consistency.

title: Progressive Overload Principle
description: Training load must increase over time to continue driving adaptation. Too little: stagnation. Too much: injury/overtraining. The art is finding the optimal incremental stress.

title: Specificity Principle
description: Training adaptations are specific to the type of training performed. Marathon runners must run at marathon pace; speed work alone won't prepare for 42.2km.

title: Individual Differences
description: Two athletes with the same VDOT respond differently to training. Recovery rates, psychological profiles, life stress, and injury history all modulate the optimal training programme.

title: Reversibility Principle
description: Detraining begins within 2 weeks of inactivity. Aerobic adaptations are lost faster than strength. After illness or injury, volume and intensity must be rebuilt gradually.

title: Coaching Communication
description: Clear, timely feedback. Ask before telling. Reflective listening. Avoid jargon. Celebrate process wins. Address performance dips with curiosity, not judgement.

title: Training Plan Periodisation
description: Macro (annual), meso (4-week block), and micro (weekly) planning. Each mesocycle has a specific purpose: base, build, peak, recovery. Plans must be adapted, not blindly followed.

title: The Art of Backing Off
description: Knowing when to reduce load is as important as knowing when to increase it. External stressors (work, travel, illness) reduce training tolerance. Life load + training load = total load.

title: Behavioural Pattern Recognition
description: Identifying recurring patterns in an athlete's choices and responses: under-fuelling before long runs, sleeping less before key sessions, catastrophising after bad workouts.

title: Feel Score Interpretation
description: Subjective effort/feel ratings (1–5) are highly predictive of training quality and recovery state. A consistent pattern of 2s and 3s signals accumulated fatigue before objective markers appear.

title: Coaching the Whole Person
description: Athletic performance is embedded in life context. Sleep quality, nutrition habits, stress levels, relationships, and work demands all interact with training. Elite coaching addresses all dimensions.

title: Long-Term Athlete Development
description: Most recreational athletes peak performance at 5–10 years of consistent training. Annual progression of 5–10% in marathon time is typical. Avoid short-cut approaches that sacrifice health.

title: Evidence-Based Coaching
description: Prioritise peer-reviewed research and large-scale observational data over anecdote. When evidence is absent, apply first principles and monitor the individual's response.

title: Intrinsic vs Extrinsic Motivation
description: Intrinsic motivators (love of running, mastery, challenge) produce more sustainable behaviour than extrinsic (trophies, social approval). Build intrinsic motivation through competence and autonomy.

title: Managing Expectations
description: Most athletes overestimate short-term gains and underestimate long-term gains. Setting realistic race-day goals based on current fitness prevents demoralisation and pacing errors.

title: Feedback Loops in Coaching
description: Fast feedback (daily feel score, HR data) enables real-time adjustments. Slow feedback (race results, quarterly VDOT tests) informs longer strategic decisions. Both are essential.

title: Coaching the Comeback
description: Athletes returning from significant injury or time off have preserved aerobic memory but deconditioned musculoskeletal tissue. Run volume must lag behind cardiovascular capacity.

title: Group vs Individual Training
description: Group training enhances motivation and pushes pace. Individual training allows exact prescription. Both have a role: group for tempo, long runs, races; individual for easy days and precise interval work.

title: Athlete Autonomy
description: Athletes who understand the 'why' behind workouts show better adherence and adaptation. Explain the purpose of each session type. Involve athletes in goal-setting and plan adaptations.

title: Periodised Coaching Conversations
description: Coaching conversations should mirror the training phase: motivational in base phase; tactical in build; confidence-building in taper; process-focused on race day; reflective in recovery.

title: Training Monotony
description: Variety in training (different surfaces, routes, session types) prevents psychological burnout and stimulates different physiological adaptations. Rigid adherence to one format leads to stagnation.

title: Transition Periods
description: After a goal race, a 2–4 week unstructured transition period (run by feel, no targets) maintains fitness while restoring motivation. Essential before beginning the next macro cycle.

title: Coaching Ethical Standards
description: Confidentiality, do no harm, informed consent for all interventions, appropriate boundaries, and referral to specialists (dietitians, physios, psychologists) when outside coaching scope.

title: Pre-Race Anxiety Management
description: Moderate arousal improves performance (Yerkes-Dodson). Strategies: controlled breathing (box breathing, 4-7-8), visualisation, process focus, routine replication. Distinguish excitement from anxiety.

title: Self-Talk Strategies
description: Instructional self-talk ('quick feet', 'tall spine') improves technical performance. Motivational self-talk ('you've got this') improves endurance performance. Negative self-talk doubles perceived effort.

title: Visualisation / Mental Rehearsal
description: Mental rehearsal of race scenarios activates the same neural pathways as physical execution. Practice specific scenarios: overtaking at 35K, running through a bad patch, crossing the line.

title: Mindfulness in Endurance Sport
description: Body-scan awareness during runs prevents dissociation and improves pacing by maintaining contact with physical signals. Mindful awareness of breath, cadence, and form reduces perceived effort.

title: Goal Orientation
description: Task/mastery orientation (improve against own standard) produces greater resilience than ego/outcome orientation (beat others). Helps athletes maintain motivation during poor performances.

title: Psychological Periodisation
description: Mental skills training should parallel physical training. Base phase: identity and values clarification. Build phase: race-specific mental skills. Taper: confidence building and race rehearsal.

title: Identity as a Runner
description: Athletes who identify as 'a runner' (vs 'someone who runs') are more consistent. Identity-based habit formation ('this is who I am') outperforms motivation-based ('I want to') for adherence.

title: Dealing with Bad Training Runs
description: Single bad sessions are noise; patterns are signal. Catastrophising a bad run worsens the psychological impact. Treat each run as data. Attribution: effort within control, conditions outside.

title: Performance Anxiety in Racing
description: Pre-race butterflies are normal and can be reframed as readiness. Excessive anxiety: breathing protocols, pre-race ritual, focus on process goals. Distinguish anxiety from excitement.

title: Overcoming the Wall Mentally
description: At 30K, when glycogen drops, the urge to quit is partly psychological. Strategies: focus on next km only, use mantras ('relentless forward motion'), count strides to 100.

title: Post-Race Debrief
description: Structured reflection (what went well, what to improve, what to maintain) within 24–48h of a race converts experience into coaching insight. Avoid emotional over-reaction in either direction.

title: Resilience Building
description: Resilience is built through exposure to difficulty within a training context. Include challenging sessions (long intervals in rain, hilly long runs) to build confidence in adversity.

title: Intrinsic Reward Systems
description: Celebrate process wins: consistency streaks, feel score improvement, personal records in training sessions. Intrinsic rewards sustain motivation better than external validation.

title: Burnout Recognition and Prevention
description: Signs: loss of motivation, dread of training, irritability, declining performance despite adequate training. Prevention: recovery weeks, non-running activities, addressing root causes.

title: Fear of Failure and Racing
description: Fear of failing to hit a goal time prevents athletes from racing boldly. Reframe: every race is practice for the next. Set confidence goals alongside performance goals.

title: Cadence Optimisation
description: Optimal running cadence is individual but 170–180 spm minimises overstriding for most runners. Increasing cadence by 5–10% reduces ground contact time and vertical oscillation.

title: Foot Strike Pattern
description: Forefoot, midfoot, and rearfoot striking are all viable. Overstriding with rearfoot strike increases injury risk. Mid/forefoot reduces braking force. Transition slowly if changing.

title: Vertical Oscillation
description: Bouncing wastes energy. Optimal vertical oscillation is 6–13cm. Reduce by increasing cadence, shortening stride slightly, and focusing on 'quiet' landings.

title: Running Posture
description: Slight forward lean from ankles (not waist), tall spine, relaxed shoulders, eyes focused 20m ahead. Excessive forward lean shifts load to lower back; backward lean increases braking.

title: Arm Swing Mechanics
description: Arms should swing forward-back (not across the body), 90° elbow angle, relaxed hands. Efficient arm swing counterbalances leg movement and contributes 10–15% of forward propulsion.

title: Hip Extension
description: Full hip extension at push-off is critical for efficient force application. Restricted hip extension (from tight hip flexors) forces compensation in lower back and opposite hip.

title: Ground Contact Time
description: Elite runners have <200ms ground contact time; recreational runners 220–280ms. Shorter contact = less energy loss. Improved by strength, stiffness of the Achilles-calf complex, and cadence.

title: Running Shoes and Performance
description: Stack height, drop, and flexibility affect force distribution. Higher drop shoes favour rearfoot strike; zero-drop favour midfoot/forefoot. Carbon-plated shoes improve running economy by 2–4% at pace.

title: Pelvic Stability
description: Trendelenburg sign (hip drop on single-leg stance) is a marker of poor hip abductor strength. Directly linked to ITB syndrome, runner's knee, and lower back pain. Single-leg exercises address this.

title: Stride Length Determinants
description: Stride length is determined by flexibility, strength, and coordination — not effort alone. Optimal stride for marathon is ~1.5–1.8m. Too short = inefficient; too long = overstriding.

title: Running Drills
description: High knees, butt kicks, A-skips, B-skips, carioca, ankling. Each isolates a phase of the gait cycle. Best performed at the start of a session 2–3×/week after a warm-up.

title: Plyometrics for Runners
description: Box jumps, bounding, single-leg hops develop elastic energy storage in tendons and muscle-tendon units. 2×/week; avoid within 48h of key running sessions. Improves running economy by 5–8%.

title: Heel Drop and Injury Risk
description: High heel drop (>10mm) may increase overstriding. Transitioning to lower drop should be done over 3–6 months. Achilles and calf must adapt progressively.

title: Breathing Mechanics
description: Rhythmic breathing (3:2 inhale/exhale pattern) coordinates with footfall. Diaphragmatic breathing is more efficient than chest breathing. Breathing rate naturally increases with intensity.

title: Running in Fatigued State
description: Biomechanics deteriorate significantly after 30K of marathon. Hip drop, forward trunk lean, and overstriding increase. Strength training reduces this deterioration. Practice marathon-pace running at end of long runs.
